course_description:
This course aims to provide students with an understanding of Korean popular culture through exploration of entertainment media from the post-Korean War era to present day. Through analysis of K-pop, television dramas, variety shows and films, students will examine how these cultural forms reflect and influence modern Korean society. Students will develop their critical and analytical skills through regular reflective practice and engagement with broader discussions about the future trajectory of Korean popular culture.

The Summer Semester external course offering may be cancelled unless a minimum of 20 students enrol.

This course explores Korean popular culture, with a focus on the phenomenon of Korean Wave or Hallyu ,ﾠwhich can include topics from music, film, fashion, youth culture, food, leisure activities to national figures. This course is designed to engage students with a variety of popular Korean culturalﾠtopics through a range of in-class activities, largely through the media which made hallyu ﾠpopular, that is the music, TV dramas andﾠmovies. The class will also discuss the factors that led to the phenomenon of hallyuﾠ and its global impact.

course_aims:
This course explores the dynamic growth of Korean popular culture and its social ground in contemporary South Korea. Through this course, students will be equipped with the insight and knowledge to understand the current Korean popular culture portrayed in Korean music, films, TV dramas and the youth culture in other forms. Students will have opportunities to discuss the values emphasised and developed in the culture and the images of Korean culture generated by the social emphasis on certain values.

Korean popular culture, known as the ‘Korean Wave’ or Hallyu as in other popular cultures, consists of movies, TV shows, music, entertainers, musicians, sports, news, cosmetics, fashion and cuisine. In Korea’s case, its popular culture is spread by TV shows, movies and music. Since the late 1990s Korean popular culture has become a global phenomenon. The ‘Korean Wave’ generates significant revenue and cultural pride in South Korea. Korean language skills, or enrolment in Korean classes, are not pre-requisites for this course.

learning_outcomes.description: Establish a good understanding of Korean popular culture in relation to the socio-economic and historical background
Critically think and discuss contemporary issues in Korean popular culture, such as education fever, depression, economic disparity, feminism and multiculturalism
Expand knowledge on the origin and driving force of the Korean Wave
Discuss the global impact of the Korean Wave and anticipate the future direction of Korean popular culture

assessment_details.title: In-Class Written Responses
assessment_details.due_date: 2/03/2026 - 25/05/2026 Students may choose any five sessions from WEEKS 2–13 to complete this task. Each submission will be collected at the end of the class. Note that the five submissions will be marked together at the end of the semester, not individually after each week.
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment encourages students to actively engage with weekly class discussions and lecture content. Students will respond in writing to discussion questions during class time. Each response should demonstrate understanding of the topic and provide a thoughtful, original perspective. Responses will be brief (approximately 100–150 words) and completed within the allocated time during class. Students may choose any five sessions from Weeks 2–13 to complete this task. Each submission will be collected at the end of the class. Late submissions will not be accepted, as this task is designed to assess real-time engagement. Responses will be marked based on relevance, clarity, and originality. Any copied or AI-generated content will result in zero marks and may be referred to the School’s Integrity Officer. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit your written response at the end of the class session via the designated paper provided in class. Late submissions and extensions are not permitted because this task is an in-class participation activity, and students have 13 opportunities, with only five responses required, providing flexibility and fairness.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extensions are not permitted for this assessment because it is designed as an in-class participation activity. Students have 13 opportunities throughout the semester to complete five submissions, allowing flexibility to choose sessions that suit their schedule. This structure ensures fairness and encourages consistent engagement with class discussions.

assessment_details.title: Reading Summary and Reflection
assessment_details.due_date: WEEK 4 - 20/03/2026 5:00 pm WEEK 6 - 2/04/2026 5:00 pm WEEK 9 - 1/05/2026 5:00 pm WEEK 11 - 15/05/2026 5:00 pm WEEk 13 - 29/05/2026 5:00 pm Due 5pm Friday of designated week, except Week 5's Reading Summary and Reflection, which is due at 5pm Thursday of Week 6 (due to Friday of Week 6 being a public holiday).
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment deepens engagement with core readings and connects them to lectures and class discussions. Students will complete five submissions based on designated readings from Weeks 3, 5, 8, 10, and 12 . The readings are marked in the course reading list as "Required" and "Reading Summary and Reflection item". Task (≈400 words per submission) Summary (≈150 words): Summarise the author’s central argument and key concepts in your own words. Reflection (≈250 words): Respond to two or more guiding prompts and include one quote from the reading (with page number) that you analyse in context. Guiding prompts (choose ≥2): How does the reading relate to this week’s lecture and class discussion? Which examples from Korean popular culture (e.g., K-pop, drama, film, fandom, webtoons, streaming) support or challenge the author(s)’s claims? Do you agree or disagree with the author’s perspective? Why? How does the reading help explain broader themes in Korean society and culture? Each submission is due online by Friday of the following week (e.g., Week 2 reflection due Friday of Week 3 at 5 pm) . Submissions will be marked based on accuracy, relevance, and originality. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Turnitin on Blackboard
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
This assessment is designed to foster reflective engagement with each lesson's topics and class discussions, supporting students in consolidating their understanding progressively throughout the course. The maximum extension allowed for the submission of weekly reflections is 7 days. This timeframe allows assessors to provide timely feedback before subsequent tasks are due, ensuring students remain on track with their learning progression.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_details.due_date: 30/03/2026 The In-Semester Exam is held in person during regular class hours in WEEK 6 .
assessment_details.task_description: The In-Semester Essay will be completed in person during Week 6 in your regular classroom and scheduled class time at 10am. This assessment allows you to demonstrate your understanding of course concepts and readings. The exam is closed-book; no notes, textbooks, or electronic devices are permitted. The essay question will require you to respond to a discussion prompt using quotes from the assigned readings provided in the exam paper. Y our answer should be approximately 600–800 words and include analysis that connects readings to lecture content and class discussions . Essays will be marked based on clarity of argument, integration of readings, and depth of analysis. Note: The In-Semester Exam is a hurdle requirement. Students must attempt this exam to pass the course. Failure to sit the exam will result in a maximum final grade of 3 for the course, regardless of performance in other assessments. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Final Essay
assessment_details.due_date: 8/06/2026 5:00 pm End of Semester Exam Period Monday, EXAM WEEK 1
assessment_details.task_description: For the Final Essay, students will choose one question from a list provided in Week 13 and write a 2,000-word (+/-10%) essay . The essay should incorporate lecture materials, class discussions, and at least three references from the course reading list. Proper APA referencing is required. In addition, students will submit a 3-minute video reflection (5%) explaining the key argument of their essay and how their understanding of Korean popular culture has evolved throughout the course. The video should be uploaded with the essay by the due date. Essays will be marked based on argument clarity, integration of course materials, and originality. The video will be assessed on clarity, engagement, and depth of reflection. STATEMENT ON AI & MT This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools. learning_activities.topic: Introduction: What is Korean Wave? Introduction to the course: What is the Korean Wave?; The influence of Korean popular culture around the globe. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
K-pop and Korean Wave The beginning and development of K-pop; Global impact and socio-economic effects of K-pop Learning outcomes: L01, L02
What is behind the growth of K-pop The cultural and social issues that rise along with the growth of K-pop Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
'Parasite' and Korean cinema Korean film's growing recognision around the world Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Is BTS popular in Korea as much as overseas? Recognition of Korean wave in Korea; The origins of popular culture consumption in Korea Learning outcomes: L01, L02
In-Semester Exam Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Eating Hansik, wearing Hanbok, living in Hanok Learn the 'objective culture of Korea', such as food, fashion, housing, language and how it has formed Korean culture today and its influence in the progression of Hallyu. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Korean drama in the world, from 'Jewel in the Palace' to 'Squid Game' History of Korean drama and its reception around the globe Learning outcomes: L01
Social realism in Korean TV dramas Common plots, contexts and characters found in Korean dramas; Korean society in the TV dramas Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Real life on screen: The evolution of Korean reality shows Korean entertainment shows as one of the top Hallyu attractions Learning outcomes: L01, L02
'Meokbang': Eat like Korean What is Meokbang? The collaboration of YouTube and Korean food culture Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Korean popular culture in new forms New generation, new culture and new media; Multicultural and inter-generational discourse on social media Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Heart & soul of Korean Wave: How Han, Jeong and Nunchi shape global entertainment The Korean values as a driving force for Hallyu Learning outcomes: L03, L04
